industries.name: Sulfuric Acid Production
industries.share: ~90% of all sulfur ends up as sulfuric acid — the world's largest-volume industrial chemical
uses: Contact process converts SO₂ to SO₃ over vanadium pentoxide catalyst, then absorbed in water to form H₂SO₄ — world production exceeds 250 million tonnes per year, more than any other manufactured chemical
Phosphate fertilizer production consumes ~60% of all sulfuric acid — attacks phosphate rock to liberate phosphoric acid: Ca₃(PO₄)₂ + 3H₂SO₄ → 2H₃PO₄ + 3CaSO₄
Petroleum refining uses sulfuric acid as alkylation catalyst to produce high-octane isooctane from isobutane and butylene for gasoline blending
Metal ore leaching: sulfuric acid heap-leach dissolves copper oxide ores, followed by solvent extraction and electrowinning (SX-EW) to recover cathode copper without smelting
Battery acid: 30–40% H₂SO₄ electrolyte in lead-acid automotive batteries — still the dominant battery chemistry for engine starting, uninterruptible power supplies, and forklift trucks

companies.name: BASF
companies.use: Major sulfuric acid producer and consumer at its Verbund integrated sites. Produces sulfuric acid from smelter off-gases and elemental sulfur for captive use in chemical synthesis and for merchant sale.

companies.name: Aurubis
companies.share: ~40% of European copper smelter acid production
companies.use: Europe's largest copper smelter. Produces sulfuric acid as a byproduct of copper smelting — SO₂ from roasting copper concentrates is captured and converted to H₂SO₄, making Aurubis one of Europe's largest acid producers.

companies.name: Mosaic Company
companies.use: World's largest phosphate fertilizer producer and one of the world's largest sulfuric acid consumers. Runs large dedicated sulfuric acid plants at its Florida and Louisiana fertilizer complexes.

companies.name: Koch Fertilizer (Koch Industries)
companies.use: Major nitrogen and phosphate fertilizer producer. Operates sulfuric acid plants supplying phosphoric acid production for MAP and DAP fertilizer manufacturing at its US facilities.

industries.name: Petroleum Refining & Natural Gas Processing
industries.share: All commercially produced sulfur originates here — elemental sulfur is a byproduct of desulfurization
uses: Hydrodesulfurization (HDS) removes sulfur from crude oil fractions (gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, fuel oil) by reacting with H₂ over CoMo or NiMo catalyst to form H₂S gas — driven by ultra-low sulfur fuel regulations (10 ppm limit in EU/US diesel)
Claus process in refineries and gas plants converts recovered H₂S to elemental sulfur: 2H₂S + O₂ → 2S + 2H₂O — producing the >80 million tonnes of elemental sulfur supplied to market annually
Sour gas (natural gas with >5.7 mg/m³ H₂S) must be sweetened by amine absorption before pipeline injection — Canada, Saudi Arabia, Russia, and UAE are largest sour gas sulfur producers
Alberta oil sands processing generates vast quantities of sulfur as a byproduct — Canada stockpiles millions of tonnes of solid sulfur blocks awaiting market demand
Sulfur recovery is both an environmental necessity (H₂S is lethally toxic) and an economic recovery — converting waste gas to a marketable commodity

companies.name: Saudi Aramco
companies.use: World's largest crude oil producer and one of the world's largest elemental sulfur producers. Recovers sulfur at its Shaybah, Hawiyah, and Khursaniyah gas plants from processing sour Arabian crude and associated gas.

companies.name: Shell
companies.use: Major global sulfur producer through hydrodesulfurization at its refineries and Claus units at gas processing plants. Shell's Pearl GTL plant in Qatar produces significant sulfur as a gas-to-liquids byproduct.

companies.name: Canadian Natural Resources (CNRL)
companies.use: Major oil sands producer and sulfur producer at its Horizon and Albian Sands operations. Produces elemental sulfur blocks stored at dedicated forming facilities in Alberta.

companies.name: ExxonMobil
companies.use: Major sulfur producer at its global refinery network and LNG plants. Operates Claus units converting H₂S from hydrodesulfurization and gas sweetening to elemental sulfur at its Baton Rouge, Beaumont, and Singapore refineries.

industries.name: Mining & Metals Processing
industries.share: ~10% of sulfuric acid use for leaching; additional sulfur in mineral flotation
uses: Sulfuric acid heap leaching of copper oxide ores (chalcocite, malachite, azurite) at low-grade deposits — dissolves copper as copper sulfate solution recovered by SX-EW electrowinning
Froth flotation uses sulfur-based collectors (xanthates, dithiocarbamates) to selectively hydrophobize sulfide mineral surfaces (chalcopyrite, galena, sphalerite) for separation from gangue
Pressure oxidation (POX) of refractory gold ores: sulfuric acid and high-pressure oxygen oxidize sulfide minerals encapsulating gold, liberating it for cyanide leaching
Sodium sulfide (Na₂S) and sodium hydrosulfide (NaHS) depress zinc sulfide (sphalerite) during selective lead flotation in lead-zinc ore processing
Zinc electrowinning from ZnSO₄ solution uses sulfuric acid electrolyte; nickel, cobalt, and manganese similarly refined from sulfate solutions

companies.name: Freeport-McMoRan
companies.use: World's largest publicly traded copper producer. Operates SX-EW heap leach operations at Morenci, Arizona and other sites consuming large volumes of sulfuric acid for copper oxide ore leaching.

companies.name: BHP
companies.use: Major copper, nickel, and zinc producer consuming sulfuric acid at its Olympic Dam copper-uranium mine and Escondida copper operations. Also produces sulfuric acid as byproduct of Olympic Dam smelter.

companies.name: Glencore
companies.use: Produces and consumes sulfuric acid at its integrated copper and zinc smelters in Canada, Australia, and Europe. Major seller of excess smelter acid to fertilizer and chemical markets.

industries.name: Rubber Vulcanization & Materials
industries.share: ~2% of sulfur use — small by volume but historically critical
uses: Vulcanization: elemental sulfur cross-links natural rubber polymer chains at 140–180°C, converting sticky latex into durable elastic rubber — Charles Goodyear's 1839 discovery that transformed industrial materials
Accelerated sulfur vulcanization uses sulfur with CBS, TBBS, or MBT accelerators to achieve cure in minutes rather than hours at lower sulfur loadings
Sulfur-cured natural rubber in truck tires, conveyor belts, hoses, seals, and vibration isolators where high resilience and heat resistance are required
Carbon disulfide (CS₂) produced from sulfur and methane used as solvent in viscose rayon and cellophane production and as a fumigant
Sulfur concrete (polymer sulfur binder + aggregate) for highly acid-resistant flooring in chemical plants, battery rooms, and wastewater structures

companies.name: Bridgestone
companies.use: World's largest tire manufacturer. Uses sulfur vulcanization in all natural and synthetic rubber tire compounds. Consumes sulfur and accelerators at tire plants across Asia, Americas, and Europe.

companies.name: Michelin
companies.use: Major tire producer using sulfur vulcanization. Pioneered silica-sulfur compound technology in the Green Tire (1992), replacing carbon black with silica and silane coupling agents to reduce rolling resistance.

companies.name: Lanxess (formerly Bayer Rubber)
companies.use: Major rubber chemicals producer supplying sulfur-based vulcanization accelerators (CBS, TBBS, MBTS) and antioxidants to tire and industrial rubber manufacturers globally.
